title: Activation Functions — ReLU, GELU, Sigmoid, Tanh, SwiGLU
summary: Nonlinear functions applied element-wise between linear layers. Without them, any stack of linear layers collapses to a single linear layer. They determine gradient flow, sparsity, and expressivity. ReLU is simplest (threshold), GELU is smooth (standard in transformers), SwiGLU is gated (state-of-art LLMs).
key_insights: Without activation: N linear layers = 1 linear layer (matrix product). Nonlinearity is what makes depth meaningful.
Each activation creates a different geometry of the learned function: ReLU = piecewise linear, GELU = smooth, sigmoid = bounded.
The choice affects: gradient flow (can gradients pass?), sparsity (how many neurons are 'off'?), and expressivity (how complex a function can the network represent?).
Dead neuron problem: ReLU neurons that always output zero can never recover (zero gradient). GELU avoids this.
Gating (SwiGLU): multiply two parallel pathways — one determines 'what to keep', other determines 'what to pass'. More expressive but 50% more parameters.

mathematical_core.gelu: $\text{GELU}(x) = x \cdot \Phi(x)$ where $\Phi(x) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \text{erf}(x/\sqrt{2})]$ is the Gaussian CDF
mathematical_core.swish: $\text{Swish}(x) = x \cdot \sigma(x)$ — smooth approximation to ReLU, self-gated
mathematical_core.swiglu: $\text{SwiGLU}(x) = \text{Swish}(xW_1) \odot (xW_3)$ — gated: one path gates, other provides content. Used in LLaMA, PaLM.

derivations.title: Why nonlinearity is necessary — the collapse theorem

steps.name: Two linear layers
steps.math: $y = W_2(W_1 x + b_1) + b_2 = W_2 W_1 x + W_2 b_1 + b_2 = W_{\text{eff}} x + b_{\text{eff}}$

steps.name: N linear layers
steps.math: $y = W_N \cdots W_2 W_1 x + \text{bias} = W_{\text{combined}} x + b_{\text{combined}}$

steps.name: Consequence
steps.math: Any number of stacked linear layers is equivalent to a SINGLE linear layer. Depth adds NO representational power without nonlinearity.

derivations.consequence: Nonlinearity between layers is the mechanism that makes depth meaningful. This applies equally to CNNs (ReLU between conv layers) and transformers (GELU in FFN).

derivations.title: ReLU — piecewise linear geometry

steps.name: What ReLU does geometrically
steps.math: $\text{ReLU}(x) = \max(0, x)$ folds the negative half-space onto zero. In $d$ dimensions, each neuron creates a hyperplane boundary. On one side: output = input (identity). Other side: output = 0 (projection).

steps.name: Piecewise linear regions
steps.math: A single ReLU layer with $d$ neurons partitions input space into up to $2^d$ regions. In each region, the network computes a different linear function. $L$ layers: up to $2^{Ld}$ regions.

steps.name: Expressivity from depth
steps.math: 1 layer, 100 neurons: up to $2^{100}$ linear regions. 2 layers, 50 neurons each: up to $2^{100}$ regions too, but the boundaries are MORE COMPLEX (compositions of hyperplanes). Depth gives qualitatively different boundaries, not just more of them.

steps.name: Dead neuron problem
steps.math: If $W x + b < 0$ for ALL training inputs, gradient $= 0$ for that neuron. With gradient-based learning, it can never recover. Fraction of dead neurons grows during training if learning rate is too high (large weight updates push biases negative).

steps.name: Leaky ReLU mitigation
steps.math: $\text{LeakyReLU}(x) = \max(\alpha x, x)$ with $\alpha = 0.01$. Small negative slope means gradient is never exactly zero. Dead neurons can recover.

derivations.consequence: ReLU is simple and creates interpretable piecewise-linear functions. But dead neurons are a real practical problem. Monitor: fraction of neurons with zero activation across the training set.

derivations.title: GELU — why transformers prefer smooth activation

steps.name: Probabilistic interpretation
steps.math: $\text{GELU}(x) = x \cdot P(X \leq x)$ where $X \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. The input is scaled by the probability that a standard normal is less than it. Large positive $x$: $P \approx 1$, so GELU$(x) \approx x$. Large negative $x$: $P \approx 0$, so GELU$(x) \approx 0$. Near zero: smooth transition.

steps.name: Comparison with ReLU
steps.math: ReLU: hard threshold at $x=0$, discontinuous gradient. GELU: smooth transition, gradient is always nonzero. For $x = -1$: ReLU gives 0 (dead), GELU gives $\approx -0.16$ (small but nonzero signal).

steps.name: Why transformers use GELU
steps.math: Attention scores are softmax outputs — continuous, smooth. FFN should be similarly smooth for consistent gradient flow. ReLU's hard threshold creates discontinuities that can interact badly with attention's smooth gradients.

steps.name: Gradient properties
steps.math: $\text{GELU}'(x) = \Phi(x) + x\phi(x)$. At $x=0$: $\text{GELU}'(0) = 0.5$ (vs ReLU: undefined). For large $|x|$: approaches 1 (positive) or 0 (negative), like ReLU but smoothly.

steps.name: No dead neurons
steps.math: $\text{GELU}'(x) > 0$ for all $x > -\infty$. There is always a nonzero gradient, so no neuron is permanently dead. This is the main practical advantage over ReLU in deep networks.

derivations.consequence: GELU is now standard in transformers (BERT, GPT, etc.) because: smooth gradient flow, no dead neurons, probabilistic interpretation. The cost: slightly more computation than ReLU's simple max(0,x).

derivations.title: Sigmoid and tanh — saturation and vanishing gradients

steps.name: Sigmoid shape
steps.math: $\sigma(x) = 1/(1+e^{-x})$. Squashes any input to $(0,1)$. Useful for: output probabilities, gates (LSTM, GRU).

steps.name: Gradient saturation
steps.math: $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$. Maximum at $x=0$: $\sigma'(0) = 0.25$. For $|x| > 5$: $\sigma'(x) < 0.007$. The gradient nearly vanishes for large or small inputs.

steps.name: Vanishing gradient problem
steps.math: Through $L$ sigmoid layers: gradient scales as $(0.25)^L$. After 10 layers: $0.25^{10} \approx 10^{-6}$. This is why deep networks with sigmoid/tanh don't train — gradients vanish exponentially with depth.

steps.name: Why ReLU solved this
steps.math: ReLU gradient = 1 for active neurons. Through $L$ ReLU layers: gradient scales as $1^L = 1$ (for active paths). No exponential decay. This is why ReLU enabled deep networks (2012 AlexNet breakthrough).

steps.name: Modern uses of sigmoid
steps.math: Sigmoid is still used for: gates in LSTM/GRU, binary classification output, attention gates in gated architectures. NOT as a hidden layer activation (replaced by ReLU/GELU).

derivations.consequence: Sigmoid/tanh were the original activations but cause vanishing gradients in deep networks. ReLU solved this (gradient = 1 for active neurons). GELU refined it (smooth, no dead neurons). Sigmoid survives only in gating mechanisms.

derivations.title: SwiGLU — gated activation for state-of-art LLMs

steps.name: Gating concept
steps.math: Instead of $\sigma(Wx + b)$, use TWO parallel linear projections: one produces the 'content', other produces the 'gate'. Output = content $\odot$ gate. The gate SELECTS which content to pass.

steps.name: SwiGLU formula
steps.math: $\text{SwiGLU}(x) = \text{Swish}(xW_1 + b_1) \odot (xW_3 + b_3)$ where Swish$(x) = x \cdot \sigma(x)$. Note: THREE weight matrices ($W_1, W_3$ for the two paths, $W_2$ for output compression), not two.

steps.name: Parameter cost
steps.math: Standard FFN: $2 \times d \times d_{ff}$ params. SwiGLU FFN: $3 \times d \times d_{ff}$ params (50% more). In practice, $d_{ff}$ is reduced to compensate: SwiGLU with $d_{ff} = 2.67d$ has similar param count to standard with $d_{ff} = 4d$.

steps.name: Why it works better
steps.math: The gate pathway learns WHEN to activate. The content pathway learns WHAT to output. Separation of 'what' and 'when' gives more expressive power per parameter. Empirically: lower perplexity for same model size.

derivations.consequence: SwiGLU is now standard in modern LLMs (LLaMA, PaLM, Mistral). It trades 50% more FFN params for better quality. The gating principle (separate what from when) is a general architectural idea that appears in LSTM gates, attention gates, and mixture-of-experts routing.

worked_examples.title: Numerical comparison at key values
worked_examples.note: x = -2: ReLU=0, GELU=-0.045, sigmoid=0.119, tanh=-0.964. x = 0: ReLU=0, GELU=0, sigmoid=0.5, tanh=0. x = 2: ReLU=2, GELU=1.955, sigmoid=0.881, tanh=0.964.

geometric_properties: ReLU creates piecewise linear decision boundaries — each neuron is a hyperplane fold
GELU creates smooth decision boundaries — better for gradient-based optimization on smooth loss landscapes
Sigmoid/tanh squash to bounded range — geometric contraction of the representation space
SwiGLU creates multiplicative interactions — richer function class than additive activations
statistical_properties: ReLU: output mean > 0 (positive bias), variance halved. Need Kaiming init to compensate.
GELU: output mean slightly positive, variance slightly less than input. Closer to mean-preserving.
Sigmoid: output mean = 0.5, max gradient = 0.25. Gradients through $L$ layers scale as $0.25^L$.
Monitor: activation distribution at each layer. Should be neither saturated (all near 0 or 1) nor collapsed (all near same value).
numerical_properties: ReLU: trivially cheap — one comparison. No exp() or division.
GELU: requires erf() or the tanh approximation. ~5x more expensive than ReLU per element. Negligible in context of matmul costs.
Sigmoid/tanh: require exp(). In float16, $e^x$ overflows for $x > 11$. Need clipping.
SwiGLU: two parallel matmuls + element-wise multiply. ~50% more FFN FLOPs.

related: FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS — activation choice is an architectural decision that shapes the function class
CNN.CONVOLUTION — ReLU between conv layers is what makes cascading meaningful (cascade collapse)
TRANSFORMER.SELF_ATTENTION — softmax is itself an activation on attention scores

notes: Historically: sigmoid → tanh → ReLU → GELU → SwiGLU. Each solved a problem of the previous.
ReLU enabled deep networks (2012). GELU enabled stable transformer training. SwiGLU pushed quality further.
The gating principle in SwiGLU is the same concept as LSTM forget/input gates — separate control from content.
